critic_raw: The multi-spectral CNN vs. RGB-only CNN for algal biomass prediction experiment is carefully designed with several strengths. However, concerns remain. First, the split is by year and water body, with test set being year 5. If some lakes are consistently harder to predict (e.g., turbid lakes where spectral signals are attenuated), and if these lakes happen to have more year-5 data due to sampling schedules, test performance may be confounded by lake-level difficulty rather than purely reflecting temporal generalization. No analysis of whether lake difficulty is distributed evenly across years is described. Second, the 'no hand-crafted spectral indices' decision means the CNN must learn spectral indices like NDVI and NDCI from the raw band data. While this tests end-to-end learning, it also means that the model requires more training data to discover these known relationships. Given 3,000-5,000 image-label pairs, the CNN may not fully exploit the multi-spectral information without explicit indices, potentially underestimating the true advantage of multi-spectral data. Third, the spatial patch size of 64×64 or 128×128 pixels is applied uniformly, but algal bloom spatial extent varies dramatically. A bloom covering half a lake would have very different patch representation than a localized bloom—no analysis of how patch coverage relative to bloom extent affects performance is described. Fourth, the primary metric is MAE, but for algal bloom monitoring, extreme bloom events (high-biomass tails of the distribution) are the most operationally critical. RMSE and the per-quantile calibration analysis partially address this, but a metric that specifically focuses on performance during bloom events (e.g., MAE conditioned on biomass > X threshold) would better align with the operational need.
all_issues_raised: Lake-level difficulty distribution across years not analyzed; test performance may reflect lake-specific confounds
No hand-crafted spectral indices may underestimate multi-spectral advantage with 3,000-5,000 training samples
Uniform patch size does not account for bloom spatial variability
MAE primary metric underweights extreme bloom events which are operationally most critical

round_transcripts.critic: The multi-spectral CNN experiment has concerns: (1) lake-level difficulty not distributed evenly across years may confound test results; (2) no spectral indices may underestimate multi-spectral advantage with limited training data; (3) uniform patch size does not account for bloom spatial variability; (4) MAE underweights extreme bloom events.
round_transcripts.defender: On (1): The temporal split by year distributes all lakes into each year's data; year 5 test contains all lakes, as does training (years 1-3) and validation (year 4). The concern about lake-level difficulty affecting test performance applies equally to both models and thus does not bias the comparison—both CNN models are evaluated on the same test lakes. On (2): The decision to avoid pre-specified spectral indices is intentional: it tests whether multi-spectral CNNs can discover relevant spectral relationships from data. If training data is insufficient for this discovery, that itself is an informative finding. Incorporating known indices as an additional channel is a valid extension. On (3): Bloom spatial variability is a real challenge; the 64×128 pixel patch centered on the sampling location captures the near-bloom region but not the full lake extent. Multi-scale patching is a recommended extension. On (4): RMSE and per-quantile calibration in the secondary metrics partially address extreme event performance; reporting MAE conditioned on biomass threshold is a useful addition.

round_transcripts.critic: On (1): The defense is correct that both models see the same test lakes, so lake difficulty does not bias the comparison between models. I accept this response; concern (1) is resolved. On (2): The defense frames the absence of spectral indices as a test of end-to-end learning—this is a legitimate scientific choice, but it means the experiment may not demonstrate the maximum achievable multi-spectral advantage. If the CNN fails to learn NDCI from raw bands with 3,000 samples, the conclusion would be that end-to-end multi-spectral CNNs don't outperform RGB, which may understate the value of multi-spectral data with appropriate feature engineering. On (4): The defense agrees that a bloom-event-specific metric is a useful addition; this remains an unresolved design gap.
round_transcripts.defender: On (2): The framing is explicit—the experiment tests the practically relevant scenario of end-to-end CNN learning. If the conclusion is that end-to-end CNNs don't exploit multi-spectral data well, this is a valid and important finding. Recommending a secondary analysis with expert-derived spectral indices would characterize this. On (4): Adding MAE conditioned on biomass above a high-bloom threshold as a secondary metric is a straightforward and valuable improvement.

all_issues_adjudicated: End-to-end multi-spectral learning without spectral indices may understate multi-spectral advantage — critic explicitly accepted this in R2 after defender clarified the experiment tests the practically relevant deployment scenario; concern resolved

Extreme bloom event performance not specifically evaluated by primary metric — defender acknowledged as valid extension; primary metric covers full test set; separate stratified analysis is a recommended follow-up, not a methodological flaw in stated experiment scope
